blocks:
Ayahs

لَآ أُقْسِمُ بِيَوْمِ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ (۱)
I swear by the Day of Resurrection! (1)
وَلَآ أُقْسِمُ بِٱلنَّفْسِ ٱللَّوَّامَةِ (۲)
And I swear by the self-critical soul! (2)
أَيَحْسَبُ ٱلْإِنسَـٰنُ أَلَّن نَّجْمَعَ عِظَامَهُۥ (۳)
Does man suppose that We will not put together his bones [at resurrection]? (3)
بَلَىٰ قَـٰدِرِينَ عَلَىٰٓ أَن نُّسَوِّىَ بَنَانَهُۥ (٤)
Yes indeed, We are able to [re]shape [even] his fingertips! (4)
بَلْ يُرِيدُ ٱلْإِنسَـٰنُ لِيَفْجُرَ أَمَامَهُۥ (٥)
Indeed, man desires to go on living viciously. (5)
يَسْـَٔلُ أَيَّانَ يَوْمُ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ (٦)
He asks, ‘When will be this “day of resurrection”?!’ (6)

Key Points

This Surah and Surah Al-Balad begin with the phrase «لا اُقسم» (Lā Uqsim). According to many commentators, the letter «لا» (Lā) is for emphasis, but some consider it for negation, meaning the matter is so clear that I do not need to swear. Perhaps the similarity between the Day of Resurrection (Qiyamah) and the Self-Reproaching Soul (Nafs al-Lawwāmah), both of which are sworn by, lies in the fact that in the small world of human existence there is a court called the Self-Reproaching Soul, and in the universe, there is a court called the Day of Resurrection (Qiyamah).(156)

The Noble Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله told Ibn Mas'ūd: Perform many good deeds, for both the righteous and the wicked will regret on the Day of Resurrection. The righteous will regret why they did not perform more good deeds, and the wicked will regret their shortcomings and negligence. Then He said: The Ayah «و لا اقسم بالنفس اللوّامة» (And I swear by the Self-Reproaching Soul) is a witness and proof of this matter.(157)

The Soul (Nafs) in the Quran

The soul (Nafs) in the Quran, narrations, and supplications is depicted in several forms for humans:

A) Nafs Ammārah (The Inciting Soul): which constantly commands man towards evil, and if it is not restrained by intellect and faith, it leads man to downfall and ruin. We read in the Quran: «انّ النّفس لامّارة بالسوء الاّ ما رحم ربّى» (Indeed, the soul is a persistent commander of evil, except those upon whom my Lord has mercy.) (158) This soul repeats its desires and demands until it ensnares the human. Imam Ali عليه السلام says: The Inciting Soul flatters man like a hypocrite and appears in the guise of a friend until it dominates man and leads him to subsequent stages.(159)

The Holy Quran states in Surah Yusuf (Joseph): After the brothers of Joseph left Benjamin in Egypt and returned to their father, explaining the story of his arrest on charges of theft, Prophet Ya'qūb (Jacob) said to them: «بل سوّلت لكم انفسكم» (Rather, your souls have enticed you to [something evil]). (160) In another narration, we read: After prayer, ask Allah thus: «الهى لا تكلنى الى نفسى طرفة عين ابداً» (O my God, do not leave me to my own soul even for the blink of an eye, ever).(161)

Imam Zayn al-Abidin عليه السلام, in the Supplication of the Complainants (Munājāt ash-Shākkīn), addresses the Almighty Allah: I complain to You about a soul that commands evil, rushes toward errors, is quick to commit sin, cries out when evil befalls it, is stingy when good is supposed to reach someone through it, has a strong inclination toward amusement and play, is filled with heedlessness, forgetfulness, and error, accelerates me toward sin, and makes me procrastinate regarding repentance.

B) Nafs Lawwāmah (The Reproaching Soul): which is mentioned in this Surah (Al-Qiyamah), and perhaps what is meant by it is the moral conscience. Yes, man possesses a state where, in response to committing evil or diminishing good deeds, he reproaches himself both in this world and the Hereafter. These reproaches are the same remorse and regret that can be a prelude to repentance or create a context for despair and self-defeat.

C) Nafs Mutma'innah (The Tranquil Soul): which is achieved through prayer and the remembrance of God, leading man to attain peace and assurance. The Holy Quran states in one place: «اقم الصّلاة لذكرى» (Establish prayer for My remembrance) (162) and in another place: «الا بذكر اللّه تطمئنّ القلوب» (Unquestionably, by the remembrance of Allah hearts are assured.) (163) The assured person does not fear death, is eager for martyrdom, is indifferent to the glitter and displays of the world, and is always content with Divine decrees.

Deniers of the Resurrection sometimes raised questions to deny the Day of Judgment. Among them was their saying: «من یحیى العظام و هى رمیم» (Who will give life to bones while they are disintegrated?) (164) And sometimes they openly denied the Resurrection, saying: «ان هى الاّ حیاتنا الدنیا نموت و نحیى و ما نحن بمبعوثین» (There is only our worldly life; we die and we live, and we will not be resurrected.) (165)

In response to the doubt and skepticism of the opponents regarding the Resurrection, which ultimately relates to the power of Allah, the Quran emphasizes Divine power in recreating humans. We point to some examples of this: «قادر على ان یخلق مثلهم» (He is able to create the likes of them [again].) (166) «بقادر على ان یحیى الموتى» (Allah is able to give life to the dead.) (167) «على رجعه لقادر» (He is certainly able to return him.) And in this Ayah, He says: Not only the return of the human essence, but even the creation of the lines on his fingertips is not a difficult task for Allah. «بلى قادرین على ان نسوّى بنانه» (Yes, [We are] able to restore his fingertips.)

Every human's fingers have specific lines unique to him, and no two people can be found whose fingerprint lines are identical. For this reason, fingerprinting is used to identify criminals. Yes, the knowledge of Allah and His power encompass the most subtle particles of every individual's existence. «نسوّى بنانه» (We restore his fingertips.)

(156) Tafsir Namūneh. (157) Bihār, vol. 74, p. 104. (158) Yusuf, 53. (159) Ghurar al-Hikam. (160) Yusuf, 83. (161) Kāfī, vol. 3, p. 346. (162) Tā Hā, 14. (163) Ra'd, 28. (164) Yā Sīn, 78. (165) Mu'minūn, 37. (166) Isrā', 99. (167) Ahqāf, 33.

Messages

  1. In the sight of Allah, the occurrence of the Resurrection is certain, and thus He swears by it. «لا اقسم بیوم القیامة»
  2. One of the characteristics of man is possessing a moral conscience, the innate recognition of good and evil, and self-reproach or the reproach of others when committing evil. «النفس اللوّامة»
  3. Among the three souls—Ammārah, Mutma'innah, and Lawwāmah—the one that resembles the reproaches and regrets of the Day of Resurrection is the Nafs Lawwāmah. «لااقسم بیوم القیامة... بالنفس اللوّامة»
  4. Belief in the Resurrection prevents committing immorality and transgression. Therefore, those who intend to commit sin and transgression question the Resurrection. «بل یرید الانسان لیفجر امامه یسئل ایّان یوم القیامة»
  5. Deniers of the Resurrection deny the Day of Judgment based on assumption and imagination, not on reasoning, evidence, or proof. «أ یحسب الانسان ان لن نجمع عظامه»
  6. The disintegrated and scattered limbs, organs, and bones are capable of being gathered on the Day of Resurrection. «نجمع عظامه»
  7. The Resurrection is physical, and on the Day of Judgment, the human existence is composed of his own bones. «نجمع عظامه»
  8. It is permissible to quote false assumptions and refute them. «أیحسب... بلى قادرین»
  9. The creation of Allah possesses order and system, both in this world: «الّذى خلق فسوّى» (168) and in the Hereafter: «نسوّى بنانه»
  10. The human of the Resurrection is the same human of this world. «بلى قادرین على ان نسوّى بنانه»
  11. Allah has granted man the power of choice, free will, and volition, and he can decide contrary to his intellect, nature, and awareness. «بل یرید الانسان لیفجر امامه»
  12. Many of those who deny the Resurrection do not have a scientific problem; rather, psychologically, their denial is so that they can, in their imagination, clear the way for their desires. «یرید الانسان لیفجر امامه»

(168) A'lā, 2.

Explanations

Portrait of Surah Al-Qiyamah

This Surah has forty Ayahs and was revealed in Mecca. Its name is derived from the first Ayah and means the Resurrection. The central theme of this Surah, as its name suggests, is the subject of the Resurrection and the Day of Judgment. It first addresses the severe and terrifying events at the end of this world, followed by the cheerful and sorrowful faces of the righteous and the wicked. The continuation of the Surah describes the states of man during the moment of death and ends with a reference to the creation of man from a drop of despicable water, which is a sign of Allah's power to recreate man. However, the four middle Ayahs of the Surah concern the Quran and the manner of its revelation and recitation. In narrations, for the one who consistently recites this Surah and acts upon it, passing over the Sirāt (Bridge) and presence on the Day of Resurrection with a cheerful and smiling face is guaranteed.

Ayahs

فَإِذَا بَرِقَ ٱلْبَصَرُ (۷)
But when the eyes are dazzled, (7)
وَخَسَفَ ٱلْقَمَرُ (۸)
the moon is eclipsed, (8)
وَجُمِعَ ٱلشَّمْسُ وَٱلْقَمَرُ (۹)
and the sun and the moon are brought together, (9)
يَقُولُ ٱلْإِنسَـٰنُ يَوْمَئِذٍ أَيْنَ ٱلْمَفَرُّ (۱٠)
that day man will say, ‘Where is the escape [from this day]?’ (10)
كَلَّا لَا وَزَرَ (۱۱)
No indeed! There will be no refuge! (11)
إِلَىٰ رَبِّكَ يَوْمَئِذٍ ٱلْمُسْتَقَرُّ (۱۲)
That day the [final] goal will be toward your Lord. (12)
يُنَبَّؤُا۟ ٱلْإِنسَـٰنُ يَوْمَئِذٍۭ بِمَا قَدَّمَ وَأَخَّرَ (۱۳)
That day man will be informed about what [works] he had sent ahead [to the scene of judgement] and [the legacy that he had] left behind. (13)
بَلِ ٱلْإِنسَـٰنُ عَلَىٰ نَفْسِهِۦ بَصِيرَةٌ (۱٤)
Indeed, man is a witness to himself, (14)
وَلَوْ أَلْقَىٰ مَعَاذِيرَهُۥ (۱٥)
though he should offer excuses [to justify his failings]. (15)

Key Points

The word barq (برق), when attributed to the eye, means a state arising from intense fear and terror. Wazar (وَزَرْ) means a refuge like a mountain or a fortress. Man's search for refuge on the Day of Judgment is either due to shame and modesty before Allah, or due to fleeing from accountability and reckoning, or Hellfire, or disgrace, and perhaps all of these. Ma'adhir (معاذیر), the plural of ma'dharat (excuse), means finding something that erases the effects of sin, whether the excuse is valid or invalid.

Usually, man fabricates excuses to justify his wrongdoings, and we point to examples of this in the Quran: Sometimes he says: We were heedless: «انّا كنّا عن هذا غافلین»(169) Sometimes he says: The elders and leaders of the people misled us: «هؤلاء اضلّونا»(170) Sometimes he says: Satan misled us, but Satan replies: Do not blame me, for you yourselves are at fault: «فلا تلومونى و لوموا انفسكم»(171)

Imam Baqir عليه السلام, in explaining the Ayah «بما قدّم و اخّر», said: «فما سنّ من سنّة ليستنّ بها من بعده فان كان شرّاً كان عليه مثل وزرهم... و ان كان خيراً كان له مثل اجورهم»(172) Whoever establishes a bad or good path and method, then whoever acts upon it after him, if it is bad, he will be given a punishment like the punishment of the sinner, and if it is good, a reward similar to that of the one who performs it will also be given to him.

Messages

1- The existing order of the universe collapses on the Day of Judgment. «و جمع الشمس و القمر»

2- The terror arising from the occurrence of the Day of Judgment encompasses the entire being of man:

Eye: «برق البصر» The eyes are seized by terror.

Heart: «قلوبهم واجفة» The hearts are seized by trembling.

Mind: «ترى النّاس سُكارى» You see the people intoxicated.

3- On the Day of Judgment, transgressions will be made known to the criminals. «ينبّوء الانسان بما قدّم و اخّر»

4- Man is responsible not only for the deeds he himself performed but also for the deeds that others perform after him and in imitation of him. «قدّم و اخّر»

5- Man's conscience is the best witness against him in the court of the Day of Judgment. «بل الانسان على نفسه بصیرة»

6- Man himself does not believe in the excuses he presents. «و لو القى معاذيرهُ»

Ayahs

لَا تُحَرِّكْ بِهِۦ لِسَانَكَ لِتَعْجَلَ بِهِۦٓ (۱٦)
Do not move your tongue with it to hasten it. (16)
إِنَّ عَلَيْنَا جَمْعَهُۥ وَقُرْءَانَهُۥ (۱۷)
Indeed it is up to Us to put it together and to recite it. (17)
فَإِذَا قَرَأْنَـٰهُ فَٱتَّبِعْ قُرْءَانَهُۥ (۱۸)
And when We have recited it, follow its recitation. (18)
ثُمَّ إِنَّ عَلَيْنَا بَيَانَهُۥ (۱۹)
Then, its exposition [also] lies with Us. (19)

Key Points

In Ayah 114 of Surah Taha, we read similar verses: «لا تعجل بالقرآن من قبل ان یقضى الیك وحیه» (Do not hasten with the Qur'an before its revelation to you is concluded). The Qur'an was revealed all at once to the Noble Prophet صلى الله علیه وآله on Laylat al-Qadr (the Night of Decree), and then, over the 23 years of His prophethood, it was revealed gradually to Him again on various occasions. Therefore, the Ayah «لا تحرّك به لسانك» (Do not move your tongue with it) indicates that the Noble Prophet already knew the verses of the Qur'an, and for this reason, Allah says: Do not hasten in its recitation, and follow its recitation after We have recited it. «فاذا قرأناه فاتبع قرآنه» (So when We recite it, follow its recitation). A portion of the verses of the Qur'an concerns the manner of the revelation of the Divine Message to the Prophet and its recitation to the people. In these verses, too, Allah tells His Prophet: When the revelation descends, do not hasten to recite it before the verses are complete. And do not rush My words, and do not worry that it might slip from your memory and you might forget it, because its preservation is also Our responsibility. So, let the revelation be completed, and then recite those verses.

Messages

1- Listening to speech has etiquette; even repeating the speaker's words is not permissible (while they are speaking). «لا تحرّك به...»

2- The Qur'an is immune from any kind of distortion or alteration. «انّ علینا جمعه...»

3- The words (wording) of the Qur'an are from Allah. «قرأناه»

4- The clarification and explanation of the revelation for the Prophet is the responsibility of Allah. «انّ علینا بیانه»

Ayahs

كَلَّا بَلْ تُحِبُّونَ ٱلْعَاجِلَةَ (۲٠)
No! Indeed, you love this transitory life (20)
وَتَذَرُونَ ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةَ (۲۱)
and forsake the Hereafter. (21)
وُجُوهٌ يَوْمَئِذٍ نَّاضِرَةٌ (۲۲)
Some faces will be fresh on that day, (22)
إِلَىٰ رَبِّهَا نَاظِرَةٌ (۲۳)
looking to their Lord, (23)
وَوُجُوهٌ يَوْمَئِذٍۭ بَاسِرَةٌ (۲٤)
and some faces will be scowling on that day, (24)
تَظُنُّ أَن يُفْعَلَ بِهَا فَاقِرَةٌ (۲٥)
knowing that they will be dealt out a punishment breaking the spine. (25)

Key Points

Nāḍirah (ناضرة) means freshness/radiance resulting from receiving abundant blessings. As He said elsewhere: «نضرة النعیم» (173) Perhaps the intent of Nāẓirah (ناظرة) is the anticipation of receiving blessings. As stated in a narration from Imam Riḍā عليه السلام: «مشرقة تنتظر ثواب ربّها» (174) (They are radiant, anticipating the reward of their Lord.) Although this Ayah states: «الى ربّها ناظرة» (people look towards their Lord), based on the Ayah which states: «لا تدركه الابصار» (175) (Vision comprehends Him not), Allah is not seen with the physical eye, and on the Day of Judgment, people will look towards the grace of their Lord. Bāsirah (باسرة) means the gloominess or contraction of the face. Fāqirah (فاقرة) comes from fiqār (فقار), meaning the spine. Faqīr (poor person) also derives from this root, meaning someone whose spine seems broken due to destitution. The intent of fāqirah is a back-breaking calamity.

173) Mutaffifīn, 24. 174) Tafsir Noor al-Thaqalayn. 175) An‘ām, 103.

Messages

1- Questioning and inquiry require psychological analysis; questioning the Day of Judgment stems from attachment to the world. «یسئل ایّان یوم القیامة...كلاّ بل تحبّون العاجلة»

2- Love for the world is bad and blameworthy only when it is either intense: «لحبّ الخیر لشدید» (176), or when it necessitates abandoning the Hereafter. «تحبّون العاجلة و تذرون الآخرة»

3- Attachment to the world is a foundation for denying the Resurrection. «تحبّون العاجلة و تذرون الآخرة»

4- The world is fleeting. It is as if the world is rushing to its end and passes quickly. «تحبّون العاجلة»

5- One heart cannot hold two loves. «تحبّون العاجلة و تذرون الآخرة»

6- Man was created from haste: «خلق الانسان من عجل» (177). If he does not restrain himself with intellect and revelation, he will only think of the immediate world (‘ājil). «تحبّون العاجلة»

7- One method of education is comparison between disbelief (or punishment) and reward: «وجوه یومئذ ناضرة - وجوهٌ یومئذ باسرة»

8- Spiritual and psychological issues affect a person's face. The color of the countenance reveals the secret of the conscience. «وجوهٌ یومئذ ناضرة - وجوهٌ یومئذ باسرة»

9- Those who worshipped Allah alone in the world: «فادعوا اللّه مخلصین» (178), relied only upon Him: «علیه توكّلنا» (179), were humble only before Him and feared no one: «لا یخشون احداً» (180), sought help only from Him: «ایّاك نستعین», and so on—on that Day, they will also look only towards Him. «الى ربّها ناظرة»

10- Attachment to the world and abandoning the Hereafter cause sorrow and gloominess of the face on the Day of Judgment. «تحبّون العاجلة... وجوهٌ یومئذ باسرة»

176) ‘Ādiyāt, 8.

177) Anbiyā’, 37.

178) Ghāfir, 14.

179) Mulk, 29.

180) Aḥzāb, 39.

Ayahs

كَلَّآ إِذَا بَلَغَتِ ٱلتَّرَاقِىَ (۲٦)
No indeed! When it reaches up to the collar bones (26)
وَقِيلَ مَنْ ۜ رَاقٍ (۲۷)
and it is said, ‘Who will take him up?’ (27)
وَظَنَّ أَنَّهُ ٱلْفِرَاقُ (۲۸)
and he knows that it is the [time of] parting, (28)
وَٱلْتَفَّتِ ٱلسَّاقُ بِٱلسَّاقِ (۲۹)
and each shank clasps the other shank, (29)
إِلَىٰ رَبِّكَ يَوْمَئِذٍ ٱلْمَسَاقُ (۳٠)
that day he shall be driven toward your Lord. (30)
فَلَا صَدَّقَ وَلَا صَلَّىٰ (۳۱)
He neither confirmed [the messages of Allah], nor did he pray, (31)
وَلَـٰكِن كَذَّبَ وَتَوَلَّىٰ (۳۲)
but denied [them] and turned away, (32)
ثُمَّ ذَهَبَ إِلَىٰٓ أَهْلِهِۦ يَتَمَطَّىٰٓ (۳۳)
and went back swaggering to his family. (33)
أَوْلَىٰ لَكَ فَأَوْلَىٰ (۳٤)
So, woe to you! Woe to you! (34)
ثُمَّ أَوْلَىٰ لَكَ فَأَوْلَىٰٓ (۳٥)
Again, woe to you! Woe to you! (35)

Key Points

“Tarāqī” (تراقى) is the plural of “Turquwah” (ترقوة), meaning the bone that surrounds the throat. “Rāqin” (راقٍ) is originally “Rāqī” (راقى), the active participle of “Raqā” (رَقى). The incantations and remembrances (adhkār) that cause the healing of the sick are called “Ruqyah” (رُقیة). Thus, “Rāqin” (راقٍ) means anything that is a means of saving the sick person from death. The word “Ẓann” (ظنّ) sometimes comes with the meaning of certainty (knowledge), just as it has come in this Ayah: the person in the throes of death becomes certain of separation and departure (firāq).

It is narrated in a tradition that at the moment of death, wealth, children, and deeds appear before the person. Wealth says: I am out of your reach. Children say: I am with you until the grave. But deeds say: I am with you forever (181).

“Ilā Rabbika Yawma’idhin Al-Masāq” (الى ربّك یومئذ المساق) (To your Lord on that Day is the driving). This driving (being herded) is sometimes by the angels of mercy towards Paradise, and sometimes by the angels of punishment towards Hell.

We read in a Hadith: “Man taraka al-ṣalāta mu‘tamidan faqad kafar” (مَن ترك الصلاة معتمّداً فقد كفر) (Whoever intentionally abandons prayer has disbelieved) (182). Whoever abandons prayer consciously and deliberately is a disbeliever. The Quran also considers the abandonment of prayer to be the fruit of disbelief. “Fa lā ṣaddaqa wa lā ṣallā” (فلا صدّق و لاصلّى).

Some commentators consider the occasion of revelation for Ayahs 31 onwards of this Surah to refer to the arrogance and pride of Abū Jahl, but even if this is correct, it does not harm the universality of the subject. The word “Yatamaṭṭā” (یتمطّى) is either derived from “Maṭṭ” (مطّ), meaning to stretch the leg, or from “Maṭā’” (مطاء), meaning to turn one's back, both of which are metaphors for arrogance and swagger.

These Ayahs depict the scene of the death of the deviants; otherwise, according to Imam Sadiq عليه السلام, the passing of a believer is like smelling the best of fragrances (183).

We read in a Hadith: The remembrance of death kills desires, uproots heedlessness, strengthens the heart with divine promises, refines human nature and removes hardness (of heart), extinguishes the fire of greed, and lowers the status of the world in the eyes of man. This is the meaning of the saying and words of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, who said: “Fikru sā‘atin khayrun min ‘ibādati sanah” (فكر ساعة خیر من عبادة سنة) (An hour of contemplation is better than a year of worship) (184).

Imam Ali عليه السلام described the final moments of a person's life and the time of death as follows: At the moment of death, a scene occurs from which there is no return or retreat. How that which they were unaware of throughout their lives suddenly descends upon them at that moment! Separation from the world they were confident in has come upon them. They turn towards the Hereafter, the dangers of which they were constantly warned about, and what descends upon them is indescribable. The agony of dying and the regret of losing the world (and what is in it) assault them. Bodies weaken and colors change. Death gradually overtakes all their limbs, then their tongue ceases to function. They lie among their family, their eyes, ears, and mind still working. At that moment, they are thinking about what they wasted their life on and where they spent their existence. They remember the wealth they accumulated during their life—the very wealth they acquired without opening their eyes or paying attention to whether they gathered it from lawful (halāl), unlawful (harām), or doubtful sources. Now the sin of accumulating it rests upon his shoulders, and he must separate from that wealth and leave it for the heirs to benefit from. The ease and pleasure of the wealth are for others, while the trouble of it is for him, and he has put himself in pawn for it. So, out of remorse and grief, he bites his hand... Gradually, the soul is taken from his ears, and then his eyes, and the spirit leaves his body. He remains on the ground among his relatives like a corpse, whom they fearfully move away from. He neither helps the mourners nor responds to their cries. Then they carry him towards his grave and resting place, bury him, entrust him to his deeds, and depart (185).

Sometimes a person does not accept something but does not reject it either, because he is in doubt. Just as they said to Prophet Salih: We are in doubt concerning that to which you invite us. “Wa innā lafī shakkin mimmā tad‘ūnā ilayhi murīb” (و انّنا لفى شك ممّا تدعونا الیه مریب) (186). But sometimes, he not only does not accept but actively engages in denial and rejection, which indicates obstinacy and stubbornness. Like these Ayahs: “Fa lā ṣaddaqa... wa lākin kadhdhaba wa tawallā” (فلا صدّق... و لكن كذّب و تولّى).

“Aulā laka” (اولى لك) is similar to “Waylun laka” (ویلٌ لك) (Woe to you), used for humiliation and condemnation. Imam Ridha عليه السلام interpreted the Ayah “Aulā laka fa aulā” (اولى لك فاولى) thus: May the good of this world be for you, and may the good of the Hereafter be far from you (187).

“Sudā” (سُدى) means neglected or left aimless. A camel that is left untended is called “Sudā” (سُدى). The specific consequence deserved by the arrogant is not mentioned, so that it may encompass every kind of misery and misfortune. “Aulā laka fa aulā” (اولى لك فاولى).

181) Tafsir Atyab al-Bayān. 182) Tafsir Makhzan al-‘Irfān. 183) Bihār al-Anwār, Vol. 6, p. 152. 184) Tafsir Namūneh; Bihār al-Anwār, Vol. 6, p. 133. 185) Nahj al-Balāghah, Sermon 109. 186) Hūd, 62. 187) Tafsir Nūr al-Thaqalayn.

Messages

  1. Depicting the moments of dying is the best means for abandoning attachment to the world. “Tuḥibbūna al-‘ājilah... Kallā idhā balaghati at-tarāqī” (تحبّون العاجلة... كلاّ اذا بلغت التراقى).

  2. The most difficult state of dying is when the soul reaches the throat. “Kallā idhā balaghati at-tarāqī” (كلّا اذا بلغت التراقى).

  3. Death is incurable. A time comes when everyone expresses helplessness before the power of Allah and voices their despair. “Qīla man rāq” (قیل من راق).

  4. Man’s attachment to the world is such that even at the moment of death, he has a presumption of departure, not certainty. “Ẓanna annahu al-firāq” (ظنّ انّه الفراق).

  5. Those who love the world and forget the Hereafter must know that one day they must separate from their beloved. “Tuḥibbūna al-‘ājilah... innahu al-firāq” (تحبّون العاجلة... انّه الفراق).

  6. The moment of death is the time of separation and departure: separation from children, wealth, status, resources, and so on. “Wa ẓanna annahu al-firāq” (و ظنّ انّه الفراق).

  7. After death, the soul remains. (The word firāq means separation, not annihilation). “Wa ẓanna annahu al-firāq” (و ظنّ انّه الفراق).

  8. The moment of death is an extremely difficult and unpleasant moment. “Wa iltaffati as-sāqu bi as-sāq” (و التفّت السّاق بالسّاق).

  9. There is no other path except movement towards Allah and return to Him. “Ilā Rabbika Yawma’idhin Al-Masāq” (الى ربّك یومئذ المساق).

  10. Beliefs are the prerequisite for deeds. One who does not affirm the truth will naturally not pray either. “Fa lā ṣaddaqa wa lā ṣallā” (فلا صدّق و لاصلّى).

  11. Affirmation of all religious truths is necessary, and if someone does not affirm one of the essentials of the religion, it is as if he has not affirmed the entire religion. “Fa lā ṣaddaqa wa lā ṣallā” (فلا صدّق و لا صلّى).

  12. Prayer is the first sign of faith and affirmation of the religion. “Fa lā ṣaddaqa wa lā ṣallā” (فلا صدّق و لا صلّى).

  13. Distance from religion and religious duties becomes a cause of arrogance and intoxication. “Dhahaba ilā ahlihi yata-maṭṭā” (ذهب الى اهله یتمطّى). Elsewhere, we read: When criminals return to their families, they have drunken laughter. “Wa idhā inqalabū ilā ahlihim inqalabū fakihīn” (و اذا انقبلوا الى اهلهم انقلبوا فكهین) (188).

  14. In response to successive denials: “Fa lā ṣaddaqa wa lā ṣallā wa lākin kadhdhaba wa tawallā” (فلا صدّق ولا صلّى و لكن كذّب و تولّى), successive threats are necessary. (The word “Aulā” appearing four times indicates that successive humiliations must correspond to drunken arrogance.) “Aulā laka fa aulā” (اولى لك فاولى).

  15. The arrogant must be humiliated. “Yatamaṭṭā - Aulā laka fa aulā” (یتمطّى - اولى لك فاولى).

188) Al-Muṭaffifīn, 31.

Ayahs

أَيَحْسَبُ ٱلْإِنسَـٰنُ أَن يُتْرَكَ سُدًى (۳٦)
Does man suppose that he has been abandoned to futility? (36)
أَلَمْ يَكُ نُطْفَةً مِّن مَّنِىٍّ يُمْنَىٰ (۳۷)
Was he not a drop of emitted semen, (37)
ثُمَّ كَانَ عَلَقَةً فَخَلَقَ فَسَوَّىٰ (۳۸)
and then a clinging mass? Whereat He created and proportioned [him], (38)
فَجَعَلَ مِنْهُ ٱلزَّوْجَيْنِ ٱلذَّكَرَ وَٱلْأُنثَىٰٓ (۳۹)
and made of him the two sexes, male and female. (39)
أَلَيْسَ ذَٰلِكَ بِقَـٰدِرٍ عَلَىٰٓ أَن يُحْـِۧىَ ٱلْمَوْتَىٰ (٤٠)
Is not [someone like] that able to revive the dead? (40)

Key Points

This Surah began with an oath by the Day of Resurrection (Qiyamah) and ends with Allah's power to resurrect the dead on that Day. Those who deny the Resurrection have not truly known Allah, because if a person's record were erased upon death, Allah's wisdom would be called into question. As He says in this Ayah: «أیحسب الانسان أن یترك سُدى» (Does man think that he will be left neglected?), and in another place He says: «أفحسبتم انّما خلقناكم عبثاً و انّكم الینا لاترجعون» (Did you think that We had created you in vain and that you would not be returned to Us?) Do you imagine that We created you in vain and that you will not return to Us? These verses first point to the Divine wisdom in establishing the Resurrection, and then to Allah's power in the creation of man, which demonstrates the possibility of recreating him. Of course, Allah has shown other examples of His power regarding recreation in this world through His Prophets: Prophet Isa (Jesus) عليه السلام brought two birds made of clay to life by breathing into them. Prophet Ibrahim (Abraham) عليه السلام brought birds back to life by calling them after their flesh had been crushed and mixed together. The seventy people who came with Prophet Musa (Moses) عليه السلام to Mount Tur (Sinai) and died when Allah the Almighty manifested Himself on the mountain, Allah the Almighty brought them back to life again.

Messages

1- Whoever considers man to be aimless and without responsibility deserves repeated scorn. «أولى لك فاولى ثّم أولى لك فاولى أیحسب الانسان أن یترك سُدى»

2- Allah is Wise, and man is a purposeful, responsible, and obligated being; therefore, there must be a Resurrection. «أیحسب الانسان أن یترك سُدى»

3- Many of man's calculations are false. At the beginning of the Surah, we read: «أیحسب الانسان أن لن نجمع عظامه» (Does man think that We will not gather his bones?), and at its end, we read: «أیحسب الانسان أن یترك سُدى» (Does man think that he will be left neglected?). Yes, man is an imaginative being, and if he lacks the guidance of the Prophets, he deviates. «یحسب... یحسب»

4- The first creation is a sign of the possibility and capability of the subsequent creation. «الم یك نطفة...»

5- The arrogant should know that they are weak and insignificant. «یتمطّى...من منىّ یمنى»

6- Allah created man in a complete form, free from any deficiency or excess. «فخلق فسوّى»

7- Studying the stages and manner of human creation is one of the ways to understand the Resurrection (Ma'ad). «نطفة... علقة... الذكر و الانثى»

8- The balance between the genders (male and female) is a sign of Allah's power and wisdom. «فجعل منه الزوجین الذكر و الانثى»

9- If there were no Resurrection, the creation of man would be meaningless and without purpose. «أیحسب الانسان أن یترك سُدى... ألیس ذلك بقادرٍ على أن یحیى الموتى»
